g.data-narration: SARSA stands for State-Action-Reward-State-Action. It's an on-policy temporal-difference learning algorithm.
g.text: on-policy TD learning algorithm that learns
values by following the current policy.

g.data-narration: SARSA is unique because it learns while actively following a policy, unlike off-policy methods like Q-learning.
SARSA involves seven key components: Agent, Environment, Actions, States, Reward, Policy, and Value Function.
g.text: The learner that interacts with the environment
External world the agent interacts with

g.data-narration: SARSA operates in a Markov Decision Process framework where the future depends only on the current state, not past history.
The SARSA algorithm follows eight key steps from initialization to convergence, continuously improving its policy.
The core SARSA update formula uses learning rate alpha, discount factor gamma, and the next state-action value.
g.text: TD Error: δ = r + γQ(s',a') - Q(s,a)

g.data-narration: The policy is updated to select the action with the highest Q-value, gradually improving the agent's behavior.
g.text: With prob ε: random | With prob 1-ε: best

g.data-narration: The SARSA algorithm loop continuously: select action, observe reward, update Q-values, and repeat until convergence.
In this grid world example, the agent must navigate from start (S) to goal (G) while avoiding obstacles.
SARSA is on-policy (learns while following the same policy), while Q-learning is off-policy (learns from random actions).
g.text: Updates using actual next action a'
Q(s,a) + α[r + γ max Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]

g.data-narration: The learning rate (alpha) and discount factor (gamma) control how quickly the agent learns and values future rewards.

g.text: 0 < α ≤ 1 | How fast to learn new info

g.data-narration: SARSA learns safely during exploration but converges slower than Q-learning due to on-policy updates.
